solution: 
```cpp
class TextEditor {
private:
    deque<char> left;  // Characters to the left of the cursor
    deque<char> right; // Characters to the right of the cursor

public:
    TextEditor() {
        
    }
    
    void addText(string text) {
        for (char c : text) {
            left.push_back(c);
        }
    }
    
    int deleteText(int k) {
        int deleted = 0;
        while (!left.empty() && k-- > 0) {
            left.pop_back();
            deleted++;
        }
        return deleted;
    }
    
    string cursorLeft(int k) {
        string result;
        while (!left.empty() && k-- > 0) {
            right.push_front(left.back());
            left.pop_back();
        }
        int len = min(10, (int)left.size());
        for (int i = 0; i < len; i++) {
            result.push_back(left[left.size() - 1 - i]);
        }
        reverse(result.begin(), result.end());
        return result;
    }
    
    string cursorRight(int k) {
        string result;
        while (!right.empty() && k-- > 0) {
            left.push_back(right.front());
            right.pop_front();
        }
        int len = min(10, (int)left.size());
        for (int i = 0; i < len; i++) {
            result.push_back(left[left.size() - 1 - i]);
        }
        reverse(result.begin(), result.end());
        return result;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(k)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses two deques to represent the text to the left and right of the cursor. This allows efficient operations for adding, deleting, and moving the cursor. Moving the cursor and modifying text is done in O(k) time per operation, meeting the follow-up requirement. The main weakness is the use of two deques, which increases memory usage slightly, but this is offset by the high efficiency of operations.
